title: How to Navigate Psychedelic Experiences When You Have Trust Issues
body:
How to move through Psychedelic Experiences When You Have Trust Issues

1. The Personal Tangle of Trust and Psychedelics

There was a time early in my psychedelic journey when trust felt like a barricade I couldn’t climb. I’d heard stories about the liberating, healing power of psilocybin and other entheogens. I wanted that freedom. Yet, every time I sat down with those medicines, my mind fixated on one thing: “Can I trust this process? Can I trust myself to surrender? Can I even trust the people around me to hold space?”

Trust isn’t an easy thing to ask of yourself, especially if your past has been messy, traumatic, or riddled with betrayal. I’ve met so many people who want to explore psychedelics but freeze at the thought of surrender. They’ve been let down before: by people, systems, themselves. They carry that hard-earned armor into sessions, often feeling isolated in their skepticism. I get it deeply. I was that skeptic.

Trust issues don’t just vanish when you take a psychedelic. In fact, the experience can amplify those feelings. The medicine pulls you inward, stripping away your usual defenses. If you’re not ready or supported, it can feel like stepping off a cliff with no safety net. What’s on the other side? Will I be safe? Can I trust what I see and feel? These questions swirl around like ghosts in the room.

One particular session early on left me feeling raw and exposed, more vulnerable than I’d ever been. I sat in a stranger’s living room, with dim lights and soft music playing. The medicine took me to places where old fears and mistrust whispered loudly. I wanted to run, to bolt back into control, but something kept me there. That something was a very fragile thread of trust - trust in the setting, in the sitter, and, most dauntingly, in the medicine itself.

That thread became my lifeline, even when it felt thin and shaky. Over time, I learned to nurture and repair it. The process wasn’t quick or painless. It involved wrestling with my own mind, confronting shadows I’d tucked away. Psychedelic therapy, for me and many others, isn’t a magic bullet. It’s an invitation into a difficult but healing conversation with trust - both external and internal.

If you’re reading this and feeling the weight of trust issues, you’re not alone. The good news is that trust can grow, deepen, and even flourish in psychedelic spaces, with patience and the right approach. And that’s what we’ll explore here - how trust and psychedelics interact, the science behind it, the challenges you will face, and ways to move forward with care and curiosity.

2. Understanding Trust Through the Lens of Psychedelic Science

Trust isn’t just a fluffy, feel-good concept. It’s rooted in biology, chemistry, and our brain’s wiring. When we talk about trust, especially in psychedelic experiences, we’re actually discussing complex neurochemical and psychological processes. The brains of people with trust issues often show altered functioning in areas key to emotional regulation, safety perception, and social bonding.

Neuroscientists have identified the prefrontal cortex, amygdala, and the oxytocin system as crucial players in trust. The amygdala processes fear and threat signals, often hyperactive in people with trauma or mistrust. The prefrontal cortex helps regulate those fear responses, offering a rational check on emotional reactivity. Oxytocin - the so-called “bonding hormone” - facilitates social connection and trust between individuals.

Psychedelic compounds like psilocybin and LSD influence this circuitry in remarkable ways. Robin Carhart-Harris, one of the leading researchers in psychedelic neuroscience, has shown how psychedelics decrease activity in the brain’s default mode network (DMN), the area responsible for rigid self-focused thinking. This allows for more flexible cognition and can reduce fear responses from the amygdala. The medicine essentially loosens the grip of ingrained negative beliefs and defensive patterns.

Michael Pollan’s account in How to Change Your Mind highlights how psychedelics reset these circuits, potentially repairing the brain’s ability to trust itself and others. This resetting isn’t a quick fix but a neuroplastic opportunity - a chance to rewrite stories of mistrust with new, softer narratives.

Additionally, the social and environmental context where psychedelics are taken hugely influences whether trust is built or broken during the experience. The presence of a trusted guide or sitter, a safe physical space, and supportive relationships help the brain downregulate fear and encourage feelings of safety. The medicine may nudge the oxytocin system, making us more open to connection and less guarded.

Understanding that trust has a physical and chemical basis provides a grounding structure. It lets us see mistrust not as personal failure but as a survival strategy embedded in our biology and history. Psychedelics create a unique window to shift these dynamics, but such shifts require gentleness, patience, and intentional care.

3. What the Research Tells Us About Psychedelics and Trust

The scientific exploration of psychedelics and social-emotional processes has gained serious traction over the last two decades. One of the most compelling strands of research centers on how psychedelics influence trust, attachment, and emotional openness.

Roland Griffiths and his team at Johns Hopkins University have been pioneers in this area. Their landmark studies on psilocybin have consistently shown that one dose can produce increases in traits like openness and connectedness for months afterward. This shift in openness is closely tied to increased capacity for trust - both in oneself and others.

In a 2016 study, Griffiths et al. noted that mystical-type experiences facilitated by psilocybin led to lasting positive changes in attitudes and moods, often accompanied by greater social trust. The sense of unity and interconnectedness reported by participants often erodes boundaries that reinforce mistrust.

Further research out of Imperial College London with Carhart-Harris has focused on how psychedelics affect brain connectivity patterns. Their 2018 study found that LSD reduces the brain’s modular segregation, encouraging cross-talk between previously isolated networks, which might underlie increased empathy and trust.

One fascinating study published in the journal Psychopharmacology showed that MDMA, which is not a classic psychedelic but often grouped with them, boosts oxytocin levels and enhances feelings of trust during controlled therapy sessions. This has informed therapeutic models that pair MDMA-assisted therapy with trauma processing, which often revolves around broken trust caused by abuse or neglect.

Stanislav Grof’s early work, though not strictly experimental by today’s standards, emphasized the therapeutic potential of non-ordinary states to heal “existential” wounds, including mistrust and alienation. He viewed these states as opportunities for deep emotional release and re-patterning of early relational trauma.

Neuroscientific findings are complemented by clinical observations that the setting (or “set and setting,” as coined by Timothy Leary) profoundly shapes whether trust is deepened or shattered. This evidence comes straight from therapists and guides, who report that when clients feel safe and supported, they’re more likely to surrender into healing, even if deep mistrust is present initially.

4. Building a Trust-Friendly Psychedelic Practice

If you carry trust issues, starting a psychedelic journey requires extra care and planning. The first step is to create a foundation - a context where your nervous system can relax and your heart can open bit by bit, without undue pressure.

Begin with the environment. Choose a space that feels safe and familiar whenever possible. This might mean your own home or a trusted friend’s comfortable living room, rather than a clinical or unknown setting. The physical surroundings matter - soft lighting, familiar objects, absence of disruptions - all contribute to a sense of security.

Next, consider the people you involve. A guide or sitter isn’t just a body in the room; they are a container for your experience. If trust is shaky, working with someone who has a gentle, steady presence, and who respects your boundaries unconditionally is essential. Ask upfront about their experience and approach, and pay attention to your intuition when you meet them.

Preparation is another pillar. Spend time talking through your fears, expectations, and past experiences with someone you trust or in journaling. Writing your thoughts down in a Journaling Notebook Leather Bound or similar can provide clarity and help externalize worries. Mapping out your intentions - why you want to take the medicine, what you hope to learn - anchors the experience to something tangible.

When the journey starts, remind yourself that mistrust is a signal, not a flaw. If fear arises, observe it gently. Let it be part of the ride, not something to resist or be ashamed of. Breathing techniques or mindfulness practices can help soothe the nervous system.

After sessions, integration sessions with a therapist or guide are vital. These conversations help unpack the experience, process emotions, and build confidence in your own inner resources. Reading books like The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk can deepen understanding of how trauma and mistrust live in the body and mind.

Lastly, be patient with yourself. Trust may not bloom overnight, but the very act of showing up to psychedelic experiences with an open heart plants seeds that can grow strong over time.

5. Common Roadblocks to Trust and How to Work Through Them

Trust issues during psychedelic sessions often show up as fear, suspicion, or a desperate need for control. You might find yourself constantly testing the sitter, questioning the medicine, or even trying to stop the trip early. These reactions are natural protective responses, but they can also interrupt healing if left unaddressed.

One common challenge is the experience of “ego contraction,” where the familiar sense of self dissolves temporarily. For someone with deep mistrust, this can feel like losing the only anchor in a storm. You might panic, feeling like you’re losing grip on reality or that you can’t protect yourself.

Working through this requires tools to ground yourself. Anchoring techniques such as focusing on your breath, paying attention to the physical sensations of your body, or holding a comforting object can help. Sometimes, gently reminding yourself that the experience is temporary and that you chose to be in this space provides a small but vital thread of safety.

Another roadblock is mistrust of the emotions that arise. Psychedelics often bring buried feelings to the surface, including shame, anger, or grief. If you’re used to shutting down or dismissing these emotions for safety, facing them head-on can be terrifying. It might feel like the medicine is “breaking” you or exposing you to danger.

The key here is compassionate self-talk and the presence of a supportive sitter or therapist who validates your feelings rather than pushing you to “go deeper” prematurely. Sometimes it’s okay to slow down, pause, or even step out if needed. There’s no shame in that.

Resistance to trust can also stem from past trauma - trauma that taught you not to rely on others, or even yourself. Trauma-informed integration practices can help you work through this over time. Books such as The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk offer valuable insights into how our bodies and brains encode that trauma and how gentle, repeated exposure to safety can heal these wounds.

It’s also common to wrestle with trust after the session ends. You might feel vulnerable or uncertain about your insights. Journaling can be your ally here - capturing your thoughts, fears, and breakthroughs helps you process them slowly and firmly. The journey with trust is ongoing and uneven, but every step forward builds resilience.

6. Stitching Trust into Daily Life Beyond the Trip

Psychedelic experiences can open doors, but the real work happens in the days, weeks, and months afterward. For trust issues, integration is where the medicine meets reality. Bringing the lessons from the trip into your everyday relationships, habits, and mindset is where trust can actually grow roots.

Start small. Notice moments where you feel safe, seen, or connected during your day. It might be a kind word from a friend, a moment of calm in nature, or even an act of self-care like making a healthy meal. These small experiences become proof points, reminding your system that trust is possible.

Developing routines that reinforce safety and self-trust is also vital. Regular mindfulness, gentle movement like yoga or walking, and creative outlets such as painting or writing can nurture your inner environment. These practices help regulate your nervous system, making you less reactive and more open to connection.

Therapeutic relationships - whether with a counselor, therapist, or trusted mentor - offer ongoing opportunities to practice trust. Because trust issues often arise from early relational trauma, rebuilding relational trust in present-day connections is a form of corrective experience. It reminds your brain that others can be safe and reliable.

If journaling appeals to you, keeping a dedicated notebook is an excellent way to track progress and reflect on your evolving relationship with trust. The Journaling Notebook Leather Bound is a solid companion for this process - durable, inviting, and personal.

Consider that trust isn’t an endpoint but a practice; sometimes you’ll take two steps forward and one step back, and that’s part of the path. Psychedelic experiences offer windows of insight, but trust grows in daily life actions - showing up authentically, setting boundaries, asking for help, forgiving yourself and others.

Ultimately, the medicine invites you to lean into the humanness of vulnerability and connection. It reminds us that trust is messy and fragile, but profoundly necessary. As you carry the experience forward, you’ll find that trust isn’t about perfect safety or certainty - it’s about willingness to engage with life as it is, with courage and curiosity.

7. A Thought to Carry with You

Trust is strange. It’s at once the most fragile and the most resilient thing we have. In my years with psychedelic medicine, I’ve seen how it can crack wide open, only to mend in surprising and beautiful ways. But here’s the twist - the medicine doesn’t hand you trust on a silver platter. Instead, it offers a mirror. It shows you the places where trust has been broken, shadowed, or neglected.

When you face those shadows, it can feel like peeling away your armor in the middle of a storm. But inside that vulnerability lies a seed. That seed may be tiny and trembling, but it’s alive. Psychedelics invite you to sit with that seed, to water it with patience and self-compassion, to listen to it even when fear screams louder.

Trust, I’ve learned, isn’t a destination to reach - it’s a daily choice to show up, again and again, with openness and kindness toward yourself and others. It’s an ongoing dialogue, often messy and complicated, but sometimes luminous with moments of grace.

So if you wrestle with trust, know this: the psychedelic journey doesn’t ask you to leap blindly. It asks you to lean gently into your own experience, to hold your fears tenderly, and to find, in your own time, the courage to believe that safety and connection are possible - not because life promises them always, but because you are worthy of them.

That thought has carried me through many sessions and many days beyond the trip. I hope it might carry you too.
